subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 13! In this unit you will become confident with angles – how to name them, measure them, draw them, and calculate unknown angles using clever geometric rules. Angles are all around you. When Amina watches the hands of the masjid clock move towards Dhuhr, when Hassan tilts the roof of a goat shelter so the rain runs off, or when Yusuf lines up a football shot towards the goal, they are all using angles, whether they know it or not.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 6 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does this matter? Angles are the language of builders, navigators, engineers and designers. A carpenter in Kismaayo checks that a corner is a true right angle so the door will fit. A pilot flying into Nairobi steers by angles measured from north. An artist creates the feeling of depth in a drawing using angled lines. When you master angles you gain a skill used every day in real work – and you build the foundation for secondary-school geometry and trigonometry. Keep a pencil, a ruler and a protractor beside you as you read, because mathematics is something you must do, not only read.
Describe an angle as an amount of turn, measured in degrees (°).
Classify angles as acute, right, obtuse, straight, reflex, or a full turn.
Estimate the size of an angle using benchmark angles such as 45°, 90° and 180°.
Measure angles accurately with a protractor, choosing the correct scale.
Draw angles of a given size using a protractor and ruler.
Use the fact that angles on a straight line add to 180°.
Use the fact that angles around a point add to 360°.
Use the fact that vertically opposite angles are equal.

concepts.title: What Is an Angle?
concepts.explanation: An angle is formed when two straight lines meet at a point. That meeting point is called the vertex, and the two lines are called the arms (or sides) of the angle. But an angle is not really about the lines themselves – it is about the amount of turn or opening between them. Picture Hassan opening the door of a shop. When the door is only slightly ajar, the angle between the door and the wall is small. As he pushes the door wider, that angle grows. If he opens it until the door lies flat against the next wall, it…
concepts.example: This catches many learners out. If you draw a 40° angle with short arms, and then draw another 40° angle with very long arms, they are the same size angle. The long arms might look bigger, but the opening between them –…

concepts.title: Types of Angles
concepts.explanation: Sorting angles into types helps us describe them quickly. Each type covers a range of sizes, and every angle you ever meet belongs to exactly one type. Learn these ranges carefully – they appear again and again. Acute angle: greater than 0° and less than 90°. The word ‘acute’ means sharp, and these angles look sharp and pointed – like the tip of a pencil or a thin slice of mango.
concepts.example: Right angle: exactly 90°. This is a quarter turn. The corners of books, tables, windows and doorways are right angles. Two lines meeting at a right angle are called perpendicular. A right angle is often marked with a…

concepts.title: Estimating Angles
concepts.explanation: Before you ever pick up a protractor, you should estimate the angle. An estimate is a sensible judgement of size based on the benchmark angles you already know. Estimating first is a habit that protects you: if your careful measurement is wildly different from your estimate, you know at once that something has gone wrong – usually that you read the wrong scale on the protractor. The most useful benchmark is the right angle, 90°, because you can picture it easily as a square corner. Half of a right angle is 45°. A…
concepts.example: For larger angles, build on the right angle. An angle a little past square is about 120° (that is 90° + 30°). A wide obtuse angle is about 135° (90° + 45°) or 150° (90° + 60°). A straight line is 180°. To estimate a…

concepts.title: Measuring Angles with a Protractor
concepts.explanation: A protractor is the tool made specially for measuring and drawing angles. Most protractors are a clear half-circle marked with degrees from 0° to 180° around the curved edge. Look closely and you will find the important parts: The baseline (or zero line): the straight edge running along the bottom.
concepts.example: The centre point: a small cross or hole in the middle of the baseline. This must sit on the vertex.

concepts.title: Drawing Angles with a Protractor
concepts.explanation: Drawing an angle is the reverse of measuring one. Suppose you want to draw an angle of 62°. Follow these steps carefully, and your angle will be accurate: Use a ruler to draw one straight arm. Mark the vertex clearly at one end.
concepts.example: Place the centre point of the protractor on the vertex, with the baseline lying exactly along your drawn arm.

concepts.title: Angles on a Straight Line and Around a Point
concepts.explanation: Now we move from measuring angles to calculating them using rules. The first two rules are about how angles fit together. Angles on a straight line add to 180°. A straight line is a straight angle, a half turn, so any angles sitting along one side of a straight line must together make 180°. If one angle on a straight line is 130°, the angle next to it must be 180° − 130° = 50°. If three angles sit along a straight line and two of them are 60° and 70°, the third must be 180° − 60° − 70° = 50°.
concepts.example: Angles around a point add to 360°. All the way around a single point is a full turn, 360°. So if several angles meet at a point and share the whole turn between them, they must add to 360°. If three angles around a…

explorations.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.
explorations.explanation: 1) a) acute, b) right, c) obtuse, d) straight, e) reflex.

methods.title: How to measure an angle with a protractor
steps: Estimate the angle first using a benchmark such as 45°, 90° or 180°.
Put the centre point of the protractor on the vertex.
Line up one arm along the baseline (the 0° line).
Read where the other arm crosses, choosing the scale that matches your estimate.

methods.title: How to draw an angle of a given size
methods.example: You measure an angle that looks sharp and clearly smaller than a square corner. The protractor arm crosses near both 40° and 140°. Which is correct?
steps: Draw one arm with a ruler and mark the vertex at one end.
Put the protractor centre on the vertex, baseline along the arm.
Count from 0° to your angle and make a small dot.
Join the vertex to the dot, add an arc, and label the angle.

methods.title: How to find a missing angle
methods.example: Two angles sit on a straight line. One is 118°. Find the other.
steps: Decide which rule fits: straight line, point, triangle or quadrilateral.
Add up all the angles you already know.
Subtract that total from 180° or 360° (whichever the rule uses).
Check by adding your angles back to the correct total.

methods.title: Angles around a point
methods.example: Four angles meet at a point: 90°, 100°, 80° and an unknown angle x. Find x.
steps: Solution: Angles around a point add to 360°.
Add the known angles: 90° + 100° + 80° = 270°.

methods.title: Vertically opposite angles
methods.example: Two straight lines cross. One of the four angles is 65°. Find the other three.
steps: Solution: The angle vertically opposite the 65° is also 65°.
Each of the remaining two angles is on a straight line with 65°, so each is 180° − 65° = 115°, and they are vertically opposite each other so they match.
The four angles are 65°, 115°, 65°, 115°.
Check: they add to 360°.

methods.title: Missing angle in a triangle
methods.example: A triangle has angles of 47° and 68°. Find the third angle.
steps: Solution: The angles add to 180°.

workedExamples.title: Classify an angle
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 155° is greater than 90° but less than 180°, so it falls in the obtuse range. It is an obtuse angle. (It is not reflex, because reflex angles must be more than 180°.)

workedExamples.title: Reading the correct scale
workedExamples.prompt: You measure an angle that looks sharp and clearly smaller than a square corner. The protractor arm crosses near both 40° and 140°. Which is correct?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The angle looks acute, so it must be less than 90°. Of the two readings, only 40° is acute. The 140° reading came from the wrong scale. Estimating first told us immediately which number to trust.

workedExamples.title: Angles on a straight line
workedExamples.prompt: Two angles sit on a straight line. One is 118°. Find the other.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Angles on a straight line add to 180°. So the other angle is 180° − 118° = 62°. Check: 118° + 62° = 180°. ✓

workedExamples.title: Angles around a point
workedExamples.prompt: Four angles meet at a point: 90°, 100°, 80° and an unknown angle x. Find x.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Angles around a point add to 360°. Add the known angles: 90° + 100° + 80° = 270°. Then x = 360° − 270° = 90°. Check: 90° + 100° + 80° + 90° = 360°. ✓

workedExamples.title: Vertically opposite angles
workedExamples.prompt: Two straight lines cross. One of the four angles is 65°. Find the other three.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The angle vertically opposite the 65° is also 65°. Each of the remaining two angles is on a straight line with 65°, so each is 180° − 65° = 115°, and they are vertically opposite each other so they match. The four angles are 65°, 115°, 65°, 115°. Check: they add to 360°. ✓

workedExamples.title: Missing angle in a triangle
workedExamples.prompt: A triangle has angles of 47° and 68°. Find the third angle.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The angles add to 180°. Third angle = 180° − 47° − 68° = 65°. Check: 47° + 68° + 65° = 180°. ✓

workedExamples.title: Isosceles triangle
workedExamples.prompt: An isosceles triangle has a vertex angle of 40°, and the two base angles are equal. Find the base angles.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The two base angles share what is left after the vertex angle. Together they are 180° − 40° = 140°. Since they are equal, each is 140° ÷ 2 = 70°. Check: 40° + 70° + 70° = 180°. ✓

workedExamples.title: Missing angle in a quadrilateral
workedExamples.prompt: Three angles of a quadrilateral are 100°, 85° and 75°. Find the fourth.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Angles in a quadrilateral add to 360°. Add the known angles: 100° + 85° + 75° = 260°. Fourth angle = 360° − 260° = 100°. Check: 100° + 85° + 75° + 100° = 360°. ✓

workedExamples.title: Two rules in one problem
workedExamples.prompt: In a right-angled triangle one of the non-right angles is 32°. A straight line is then extended from the 32° corner. Find (a) the third angle of the triangle and (b) the angle next to the 32° on the straight line.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: (a) The triangle has a 90° angle and a 32° angle, so the third is 180° − 90° − 32° = 58°. (b) The angle next to 32° on the straight line is 180° − 32° = 148°. Two different rules, each giving one certain answer.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Three angles sit on a straight line: 55°, 65° and x. Find x.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: A right-angled triangle has one angle of 40°. Find all three of its angles.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: The smaller angle between two arms is 118°. Find the reflex angle on the other side.

activities.title: Angle Hunt Around the Home
activities.materials: a notebook, a pencil, and your sharp eyes.
steps: Walk through your home and find one example of each type of angle: acute, right, obtuse, straight and reflex.
Sketch each one and label it with its type and your estimate of its size in degrees.
Look for right angles especially – doors, windows, tables, books. Why do you think builders use so many right angles?
Share your five best examples with your teacher at your next session.
Tip: the corner where a wall meets the floor is a right angle; a slightly open door makes an acute or obtuse angle depending on how far it swings.

activities.title: Make a Paper Protractor Angle Tester
steps: Fold the square exactly in half, then in half again, to make a folded corner. Open it out.
The single fold makes a straight line – that is 180°. The double fold makes a right angle – that is 90°.
Fold once more to make a 45° corner. You now have a simple tester for 45°, 90° and 180°.
Hold your folded tester against angles around the home and decide whether each angle is smaller than, equal to, or larger than a right angle.

activities.title: The Triangle Tear (180° Investigation)
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, a pencil and scissors.
steps: Draw any triangle you like and cut it out.
Tear off the three corners of the triangle.
Arrange the three torn corners tip to tip along the straight edge of your ruler.
Notice they fit exactly along a straight line, showing the three angles add to 180°. Try it with a completely different triangle – does it still work?
This is a real proof you can hold in your hands: the corners of any triangle always make a straight angle.

activities.title: Clock Angle Game
activities.materials: a clock with hands, or a picture of one you can draw.
steps: Set or draw the hands at 3:00. What type of angle do they make? Estimate its size.
Now try 6:00, 9:00 and 12:00. Which times give a right angle, a straight angle, and a full turn?
Remember each hour mark is 30° apart (because 360° ÷ 12 = 30°). Use this to work out the angle at 2:00 and at 5:00.
Challenge a family member to name a time that gives an obtuse angle, then check with your estimate.

activities.title: Design a Tiled Pattern (Angles Around a Point)
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, a protractor and colouring pencils.
steps: Mark a point in the middle of your page. This is where several angles will meet.
Using your protractor, draw lines out from the point so the angles around it add to exactly 360°.
Try making four equal angles (each 90°), then six equal angles (each 60°). Check they add to 360° each time.
Colour the sections to make a pattern like the tiles or windows in a masjid. Angles around a point always fill the full 360°.

activities.title: Family Direction Challenge (Turns and Angles)
activities.materials: your family or friends and some open space.
steps: Stand facing one direction. Call out turns: ‘Turn 90° clockwise!’ ‘Turn 180°!’
After each turn, everyone says which way they now face and how far they turned in total.
Play ‘get back home’: give a series of turns and see who can return to their starting direction.
Talk about how four quarter-turns (4 × 90°) make one full turn of 360°.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand angles best? Which type of angle was hardest to spot? What surprised you about the triangle tear? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Types of Angles Acute: less than 90 degrees. Right: exactly 90 degrees. Obtuse: between 90 and 180 degrees. Straight: exactly 180 degrees. Reflex: between 180 and 360 degrees. Full turn: exactly 360 degrees.

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: Angle Sum Rules Angles on a straight line add to 180 degrees. Angles around a point add to 360 degrees. Angles in a triangle add to 180 degrees. Angles in a quadrilateral add to 360 degrees.

rules.title: Key rule 3
rules.text: Vertically Opposite Angles When two straight lines cross, the angles opposite each other are equal. Neighbouring angles at the crossing add to 180 degrees.

terms: The amount of turn between two lines meeting at a point
The point where the two arms of an angle meet
The tool used to measure and draw angles
Two lines that meet at a right angle (90°)
Equal angles opposite each other when two lines cross
A triangle with two equal sides and two equal angles
A triangle with all sides equal and all angles 60°
commonMistakes: Estimate first, then pick the matching reading
Thinking long arms mean a bigger angle
Angle size depends only on the opening
Adding instead of subtracting in a triangle

realProblems.prompt: A pizza in Mombasa is cut into 6 equal slices. What is the angle at the tip of each slice? What if it were cut into 12 equal slices?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Two roads cross near the Garissa market forming a 65° angle. Find the other three angles at the crossing, naming the rule you use for each.
realProblems.answer: By vertically opposite angles one other angle is 65°; the remaining two are on a straight line with 65°, so each is 180° − 65° = 115°. Four angles: 65°, 115°, 65°, 115°.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: By vertically opposite angles one other angle is 65°; the remaining two are on a straight line with 65°, so each is 180° − 65° = 115°. Four angles: 65°, 115°, 65°, 115°.

realProblems.prompt: Amina designs a triangular kite. Two of its angles are 50° and 50°. Find the third angle and name the type of triangle.
realProblems.answer: 180° − 50° − 50° = 80°; the kite outline here is an isosceles triangle (two equal angles).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 180° − 50° − 50° = 80°; the kite outline here is an isosceles triangle (two equal angles).

realProblems.prompt: A ladder leans against a wall, making a 68° angle with the ground. The wall meets the ground at a right angle. What angle does the ladder make with the wall? (Hint: the ladder, wall and ground form a right-angled triangle.)
realProblems.answer: The triangle has a 90° angle and a 68° angle, so the angle with the wall is 180° − 90° − 68° = 22°.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The triangle has a 90° angle and a 68° angle, so the angle with the wall is 180° − 90° − 68° = 22°.

realProblems.prompt: At 4:00, roughly what type of angle do the hour and minute hands of a clock make, and estimate its size using benchmark angles.
realProblems.answer: At 4:00 the hands make an obtuse angle, about 120° (each hour mark is 30°, and 4 hours × 30° = 120°).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: At 4:00 the hands make an obtuse angle, about 120° (each hour mark is 30°, and 4 hours × 30° = 120°).

realProblems.prompt: Idris walks a path that turns through 90°, then 90°, then 90°, then 90°, arriving back where he started. What is the total angle turned, and how does this relate to a full turn?
realProblems.answer: 90° × 4 = 360°, which is exactly one full turn – that is why he ends up facing his starting direction.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 90° × 4 = 360°, which is exactly one full turn – that is why he ends up facing his starting direction.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: The smaller angle between two arms is 118°. Find the reflex angle on the other side.
In a triangle, one angle is 20° more than another, and the third angle is 90°. Find all three angles.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Let the smaller of the two unequal angles be x; then x + (x + 20°) + 90° = 180°, so 2x = 70°, x = 35°; angles are 35°, 55°, 90°.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Two angles on a straight line are in the ratio 2 : 3. Find both angles.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Parts 2 + 3 = 5; each part = 180° ÷ 5 = 36°; angles are 72° and 108°.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A quadrilateral has three equal angles and a fourth angle of 120°. Find the size of each equal angle.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The three equal angles share 360° − 120° = 240°, so each is 240° ÷ 3 = 80°.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Two straight lines cross so that one angle is three times its neighbour on the line. Find all four angles.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Neighbours on a line: x + 3x = 180°, 4x = 180°, x = 45°; so 45° and 135°, and by vertically opposite angles all four are 45°, 135°, 45°, 135°.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Angles on a Straight Line and Around a Point.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Now we move from measuring angles to calculating them using rules. The first two rules are about how angles fit together. Angles on a straight line add to 180°. A straight line is a straight angle, a half turn, so any angles sitting along one side of a straight line must together make 180°. If one angle on a straight line is 130°, the angle next to it must be 180° − 130° = 50°. If three angles sit along a straight line and two of them are 60° and 70°, the third must be 180° − 60° − 70° = 50°.

The point where the two arms of an angle meet
The tool used to measure and draw angles

options: The point where the two arms of an angle meet
The tool used to measure and draw angles
Two lines that meet at a right angle (90°)

questions.question: What does “Vertex” mean?
options: The point where the two arms of an angle meet
The tool used to measure and draw angles
Two lines that meet at a right angle (90°)
Equal angles opposite each other when two lines cross

questions.hint: Use the Unit 13 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Vertex means The point where the two arms of an angle meet.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does this matter? Angles are the language of builders, navigators, engineers and designers. A carpenter in Kismaayo checks that a corner is a true right angle so the door will fit. A pilot flying into Nairobi steers by angles measured from north. An artist creates the feeling of depth in a drawing using angled lines. When you master angles you gain a skill used every day in real work – and you build the foundation for secondary-school geometry and trigonometry. Keep a pencil, a ruler and a protractor beside you as you read, because mathematics is something you must do, not only read.
I can describe an angle as an amount of turn, measured in degrees (°).
I can classify angles as acute, right, obtuse, straight, reflex, or a full turn.
I can estimate the size of an angle using benchmark angles such as 45°, 90° and 180°.
I can measure angles accurately with a protractor, choosing the correct scale.
I can draw angles of a given size using a protractor and ruler.
I can use the fact that angles on a straight line add to 180°.
